Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic on the surface, but I had my own system. I'm someone who learns best by doing things with my hands, so I'd often be working through problems physically, writing things out multiple times, or pacing while reviewing concepts out loud. I aimed for about 24 hours a week, but those hours were scattered based on when I could focus through my anxiety. Some days I'd study in intense bursts, other days I could barely sit still long enough to get anything done.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was going on in my head and what deadlines were coming up. I couldn't stick to a rigid schedule because my anxiety would spike unpredictably, and forcing myself to study during those moments was useless. I learned to work around it—studying when I felt capable and using that time intensively. Deadlines definitely pushed me to find those windows more urgently, but I couldn't predict when I'd be in the right headspace.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I needed to check if there was something due or when my peer study group mentioned something was posted. The fear of missing an important deadline was a big motivator, honestly. Sometimes I'd log in just to calm my nerves and make sure I hadn't overlooked anything. It was more anxiety-driven than anything else—I needed to verify things were okay.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became a comfortable routine for me. I'd use it when necessary, but I preferred working with physical materials or studying with my peer group in person. The times I logged in most consistently were when my study partners would remind me to check something specific. It felt more like an obligation than a habit I naturally developed.
Q5: The discussion forums were actually helpful when I engaged with them, because seeing how others approached problems gave me different perspectives. I also found the video lectures useful—I'd watch them once to get the concepts, then work through everything hands-on afterward. The peer interaction features helped me connect with my study group, which was probably the most valuable thing for my learning style since I could actually talk things through with real people.
Q6: I often ignored the supplementary readings and optional materials. With my anxiety levels, I had to be strategic about where I put my energy, and those felt like extras I couldn't afford to stress about. I also didn't complete a lot of the smaller tasks on the LMS because I was focused on understanding the core material deeply enough to perform well on exams. If something wasn't directly tied to my grade, it usually got pushed aside.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to what I'd be tested on or if it helped me actually understand something I was struggling with. I also valued anything that let me work with others or apply concepts practically. If it was just passive reading or felt like busywork, my brain would rebel against it. I needed to feel like I was building toward something concrete.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford mentally. Required ones came with deadlines and consequences, which gave me structure even when my anxiety made everything feel overwhelming. Optional stuff just added to the noise in my head—another thing I could potentially fail at or fall behind on. I know that sounds backwards, but the pressure of required work actually helped me focus, while optional work just made me more anxious about not doing enough.
Q9: Absolutely. Most of my real studying happened offline with printed notes, textbooks, and materials I'd downloaded. I'm a kinesthetic learner, so I need to write things out, highlight, make flashcards—physical stuff. I'd download lecture slides and PDFs, then work through them at my desk or with my study group at the library. The LMS was just the delivery system; the actual learning happened elsewhere.
Q10: There were many times I was studying intensively but my LMS activity showed almost nothing. I'd watch a video once, download the materials, and then spend hours working through problems by hand with my study partners. We'd meet up and quiz each other, work through practice problems on whiteboards, explain concepts to each other. None of that showed up anywhere in the system, but that's where my real learning happened.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a lecture video or PDF once, download or take detailed notes, and then never go back to it on the LMS. I'd study those materials for days or weeks afterward, but as far as the system knew, I only engaged with them briefly. My notes and downloaded files became my primary study resources. I didn't see the point in streaming things repeatedly when I could have them saved locally.
Q12: There were definitely periods when my anxiety spiked and engaging with anything felt impossible. Sometimes it was triggered by a poor assignment grade or feeling overwhelmed by how much I'd missed in class due to my attendance issues. During those times, even logging into the LMS felt like facing a mountain of evidence of my failures. The peer support intervention helped pull me back, but those dark moments were real.
Q13: My anxiety was the biggest factor affecting how I used the LMS. When it was bad, I'd avoid logging in because seeing incomplete tasks or approaching deadlines made everything worse. Fatigue from managing my mental state also meant I had less energy for engaging with online systems. I'd prioritize the bare minimum—checking what was absolutely necessary—and do my real studying in ways that felt less overwhelming.
Q14: Many times I'd log in and just stare at the screen, clicking through things without really absorbing anything. My mind would be racing with worry about whether I was doing enough, whether I'd fail, whether everyone else was ahead of me. I'd have a video playing but not actually hear a word of it. Those sessions felt like going through the motions to convince myself I was trying, even when I wasn't really present.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. My assignment completion rate was terrible, and I'm not proud of that. The anxiety made starting things feel impossible—like if I started and did poorly, it would confirm my worst fears about myself. So I'd avoid them until the fear of not submitting at all outweighed the fear of failing. It's a terrible cycle, but that's how my brain worked.
Q16: Deadlines were both my enemy and my savior. They created intense pressure that spiked my anxiety, but they also forced me to act when I otherwise might have stayed paralyzed. I'd often cram everything into the final hours before something was due, which meant my LMS activity would show these intense bursts followed by nothing. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have submitted anything at all.
Q17: Feedback was complicated for me. Good feedback motivated me and helped me believe I could succeed, which is probably why my exam scores were so high—I knew I understood the material when I got positive reinforcement. But negative feedback on assignments could send me spiraling, making me avoid similar tasks afterward. The peer support I received helped me process feedback more constructively, but it was always an emotional experience.
Q18: The course structure felt like it expected consistent, steady engagement, which didn't match how I function. I'm someone who works in intense bursts when I can manage my anxiety, not in predictable weekly chunks. The structure made my inconsistent patterns look worse than they were—my attendance and task completion rates don't reflect that I was mastering the material, just that I wasn't doing it on the expected timeline.
Q19: Having peer interaction features made engagement easier because connecting with others helped ground me when anxiety took over. But the sheer number of small tasks and regular checkpoints made things harder—each incomplete item felt like another failure piling up. If the course had focused more on demonstrating mastery through exams and less on constant small submissions, it would have suited my learning style better.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me, actually—logging in frequently out of anxiety, checking things repeatedly, watching videos multiple times to try to focus, but struggling to actually complete and submit work. The fear and avoidance cycle can create a lot of activity without productive output. Or maybe they're engaging deeply with the material in ways that don't result in formal submissions, like I did with my exam preparation.
Q21: That's basically my story. My LMS engagement was low, but I got a distinction because I learned the material deeply through offline study and peer collaboration. The exam is where I could show what I actually knew, without the anxiety of ongoing deadlines and submissions. Low LMS activity doesn't mean low learning—it might just mean someone found better ways to engage with the content that don't show up in the data.
Q22: Course logs can show when someone accessed materials and how often, but they completely miss the quality of that engagement and everything that happens offline. They can't capture the hours I spent with flashcards, the study sessions with peers, or the deep thinking I did while pacing my room. They also can't distinguish between anxious, unproductive clicking and genuine focused engagement. The numbers tell a very incomplete story.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material until it makes sense, connecting it to what I already know, and being able to apply it. For me, it's a physical and social process—writing, discussing, practicing. It's not about how many times I logged in or how many minutes I watched videos. True engagement is when I can explain a concept to my study partner and answer their questions about it. That's when I know I'm really learning.
Q24: The LMS data would never show how hard I fought against my anxiety to succeed in this course, or how crucial my peer support group was to keeping me afloat. It wouldn't capture the hours of offline studying, the late nights with flashcards, or the moments when a friend talked me down from wanting to drop out. My distinction grade came from genuine understanding and hard work—just not the kind that leaves digital footprints.
